title: Energy Considerations for EXIM Reauthorization
gist: CSIS's Ray Cai argues the US Export-Import Bank (EXIM), whose authorization expires Dec 31, 2026, has become 'the ECA of last resort'—ranked dead last among 25 export credit agencies—even as export credit agencies become central to global energy finance. China out-finances the US in public energy finance roughly ten-to-one since 2015. Cai lays out the reauthorization fight and the specific reforms (longer term, higher exposure cap, relaxed default-rate cap, looser content/shipping rules) needed to make EXIM competitive in energy and infrastructure deals.

insights.claim: EXIM's own 2025 competitiveness report calls the bank 'the ECA of last resort', and US exporters ranked it last among 25 export credit agencies in both 2023 and 2024, worst on customer service, deal-execution speed, and product offerings.
insights.speaker: Ray Cai
insights.so_what: A structurally uncompetitive EXIM means US energy/infrastructure exporters lose deals to foreign-backed rivals; reform is the catalyst to watch.
insights.quote: EXIM's own 2025 competitiveness report offers an unsparing assessment that the bank has become “the ECA of last resort.”
insights.locator: Q2: How competitive is EXIM relative to peer ECAs?

insights.claim: In 2024 EXIM's $5.9 billion in medium- and long-term commitments trailed not only China's $23.5 billion but also Italy's $16.9 billion, showing the US underwriting far less export finance than smaller economies.
insights.speaker: Ray Cai
insights.so_what: Quantifies US export-finance underprovision versus peers; a reauthorized, scaled-up EXIM would shift competitive dynamics for US capital-goods exporters.
insights.quote: In 2024, EXIM's $5.9 billion in medium- and long-term commitments not only lagged far behind China's $23.5 billion but also that of ECAs of smaller economies, such as Italy's $16.9 billion.

insights.claim: China has outpaced the United States in public energy finance globally by nearly ten-to-one since 2015, increasingly via smaller energy projects in riskier emerging markets and a more diversified mix of SOEs, private firms, and policy banks.
insights.speaker: Ray Cai
insights.so_what: China's 10:1 energy-finance lead entrenches its supplier position in emerging-market energy infrastructure; a key strategic gap for US policy and a tailwind for Chinese exporters.
insights.quote: China has outpaced the United States in public energy finance globally by nearly ten to one since 2015.
insights.locator: Q5 / This shift is also evident outside the OECD

insights.claim: Clean energy has surpassed fossil fuels as a share of global export-credit-agency energy finance, rising from under 30 percent a decade ago to a majority of OECD-Arrangement commitments since 2021.
insights.speaker: Ray Cai
insights.so_what: ECA capital is now majority clean-energy; positioning US export finance away from this stream risks ceding the fastest-growing segment of global energy finance.
insights.quote: Clean energy has also surpassed fossil fuels as a share of global ECA energy finance, rising from less than 30 percent a decade ago to constitute a majority of commitments since 2021.

themes.theme: Lapse risk and the productivity penalty

insights.claim: An NBER study found EXIM financing fell nearly 85 percent between 2014 and 2019 during its quorum lapse, and firms that lost EXIM access saw an 18 percent drop in global sales plus permanent cuts to capital and employment—not mere market-share reshuffling.
insights.speaker: Ray Cai
insights.so_what: A reauthorization lapse causes real, permanent damage to US exporters' output and jobs; the Dec 31 2026 deadline is a hard catalyst with asymmetric downside.
insights.quote: Firms that had previously relied on EXIM experienced an 18 percent drop in global sales during the shutdown; this in turn led to permanent reductions in capital and employment
insights.locator: Q3 / Recent history underscores the stakes

insights.claim: The harm from losing EXIM support fell hardest on the most productive firms, which suffered sales losses roughly five times larger than less productive peers.
insights.speaker: Ray Cai
insights.so_what: A lapse disproportionately damages high-productivity US exporters—the very firms with the most competitive global standing.
insights.quote: these firms experienced sales losses roughly five times larger than those of their less productive peers.

insights.claim: EXIM authorization expires December 31, 2026, with the annual NDAA seen as the likely legislative vehicle; Chairman Jovanovic wants a narrow renewal first while the White House seeks a broader package emphasizing priority sectors.
insights.speaker: Ray Cai
insights.so_what: Track the NDAA cycle as the reauthorization timeline; the narrow-vs-broad split is the key legislative uncertainty.
insights.quote: the annual National Defense Authorization Act is viewed as a likely legislative vehicle for reauthorization.
insights.locator: Q3: What is the legislative state of play?

insights.claim: EXIM's unique 2 percent default-rate cap and stop-work trigger deters higher-risk strategic deals—a single nuclear default could breach the cap and force a shutdown—so Cai suggests raising the cap or carving out a national-interest account.
insights.speaker: Ray Cai
insights.so_what: The default-cap reform is decisive for whether EXIM can finance capital-intensive nuclear/energy deals; watch for a national-interest carveout.
insights.quote: A single nuclear default, for instance, could be enough to breach the cap and trigger a shutdown.
insights.locator: Q4 / A related set of questions concerns EXIM's strategic agility

insights.claim: Exporters view EXIM's US-flag shipping requirement and 85 percent domestic-content rule as too rigid for modern supply chains, a key disadvantage versus foreign ECAs with looser rules.
insights.speaker: Ray Cai
insights.so_what: Relaxing content/shipping rules would directly widen the pool of EXIM-financeable deals; a concrete reform lever to monitor.
insights.quote: Exporters view EXIM's U.S. flag shipping requirements and 85 percent domestic content rules as overly rigid for modern supply chains

insights.claim: EXIM's 2019 reauthorization mandated renewables be at least 5 percent of financing authority, and in 2024 it backed a $1.6B Angola solar-storage mini-grid loan, a $98M Romania small-modular-reactor study loan, and a $297M Iraq waste-heat plant guarantee.
insights.speaker: Ray Cai
insights.so_what: Shows the concrete energy deal types EXIM finances (SMRs, mini-grids, LNG, nuclear)—the addressable pipeline a scaled-up bank would expand.
insights.locator: Q1 / Energy has emerged as a strategic focus for EXIM

insights.claim: Chairman Jovanovic in March 2026 named 'ensuring American energy molecules and technologies reach every corner of the globe' a top-four EXIM priority, and the bank recently backed $2B of LNG export-credit insurance in Egypt and a Poland nuclear plant credit agreement.
insights.speaker: Ray Cai
insights.so_what: Signals EXIM tilting toward fossil-energy ('molecules') and nuclear exports—relevant for US LNG and nuclear exporters seeking financing.
insights.quote: ensuring American energy molecules and technologies reach every corner of the globe
